metadata.title: What the Research Actually Says About Study Techniques
metadata.note: CLI video in the REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(slate) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Students spend more time re-reading their notes than on any other study activity. Cognitive science research rates re-reading as low utility. That gap — between what students do and what the research says works — is costing grades and compressing learning into the last 48 hours before every exam. One synthesis can close it.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'Students study for hours. Most of it does not stick.' — re-reading as the default vs retrieval as the evidence choice; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: One command. Ask Claude to synthesize the cognitive-science research on five study techniques — re-reading, highlighting, spaced practice, interleaved practice, and retrieval practice — and format the output as a five-row evidence table with primary research support, comparative effectiveness, and the reason students avoid each high-utility technique.

props.command: claude "Synthesize the cognitive-science research on 5 study techniques: re-reading, highlighting, spaced practice, interleaved practice, and retrieval/practice testing. For each: primary research support, comparative effectiveness vs baseline, one reason students avoid it. Format as a 5-row markdown table. Cite Dunlosky et al. 2013. Flag unverifiable claims."

beats.narration_text: The output is a markdown file — study techniques brief dot md. Five rows. The table surfaces the research utility rating from Dunlosky and colleagues 2013, which ranked ten learning techniques on a three-point scale. Retrieval practice and spaced practice are the only two rated high utility. Re-reading and highlighting are rated low.

props.code:
# study_techniques_brief.md — evidence table output

| Technique          | Primary Support         | Effectiveness | Students Avoid Because      |
|--------------------|-------------------------|---------------|-----------------------------|
| Re-reading         | Dunlosky et al. 2013    | LOW           | Feels productive (fluency)  |
| Highlighting       | Dunlosky et al. 2013    | LOW           | Fast, low-effort, visible   |
| Spaced Practice    | Cepeda et al. 2006      | HIGH          | Requires planning ahead     |
| Interleaved Prac.  | Rohrer & Taylor 2007    | MODERATE-HIGH | Feels harder than blocking  |
| Retrieval/Testing  | Roediger & Karpicke 06  | HIGH          | Feels like failing, not studying |

⚠ Flag: exact effect sizes vary by domain — treat as directional, not exact.

beats.narration_text: The table lands. The pattern is stark. The two techniques students most frequently use — re-reading and highlighting — are the two rated lowest utility. The two rated highest — spaced practice and retrieval — are the two students most frequently avoid.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording of the five-row markdown table in terminal — LOW rows in one visual cluster, HIGH rows in another

beats.narration_text: Now make it practical. Ask Claude to add a column — 'How to start this week' — with one specific action per technique that a first-semester student can implement before the next exam, with no additional materials required.

props.command: claude "Add a 'How to start this week' column to the study techniques table — one concrete, no-prep action per technique a first-semester student can implement before their next exam. Keep the Dunlosky utility ratings."

beats.narration_text: The revised table adds the implementation column. Retrieval practice becomes: close the notes, write what you remember from today's lecture. Spaced practice becomes: set three 45-minute review sessions across the next week in your calendar now. Neither requires buying anything or downloading anything.
beats.visual_intent: screen recording of updated table with 'How to start this week' column — spaced and retrieval rows fully visible with specific actions

beats.narration_text: The research pattern from Dunlosky and colleagues is twenty years of cognitive science compressed to one table: the techniques that feel most like studying are the least effective. The techniques that feel like testing — and feel harder — are the ones that actually transfer to exam performance.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'Fluency ≠ learning. Difficulty = signal.' — HIGH utility = retrieval + spacing; LOW utility = re-reading + highlighting

beats.narration_text: Your move: replace one re-reading session this week with a blank-page recall session. Close the notes, write everything you remember on a blank page, then check. The discomfort you feel is the learning happening. Track whether your exam scores change.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) swap one re-read for blank-page recall, 2) add two spaced sessions to calendar, 3) track exam score change

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
